text: The surgeon begins the procedure by making an incision along the anovaginal line, gaining access to the avascular rectovaginal septum.
The surgeon identifies and mobilizes the two stumps of the interrupted external sphincter, carefully manipulating these structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon overlaps the two stamps of the interrupted external sphincter and sutures them using interrupted PROLINE sutures.
The surgeon performs a suture on the anterior anal wall beneath the sphincter to close the visible defect.
The surgeon performs plastic reconstruction using a pedicle pudendal fasciocutaneous flap, visually manipulating the flap to cover the defect area.
The surgeon demonstrates the vertical volvoperineal flap, which is vascularized by the terminal branches, highlighting the flap's blood supply.
The surgeon identifies the internal pudendal artery, which supplies blood to the vertical volvoperineal flap.
The surgeon measures the skin flap, which is three to five centimeters wide and seven to twelve centimeters long, and delimits it along the genitocrural fold.
The middle edge of the pedicle pudendal fasciocutaneous flap is positioned laterally to the labia majora, indicating its orientation in relation to surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon makes an incision for the flap, cutting through the skin, subcutaneous tissue, and the fascia of the adductor muscle.
The surgeon lifts the flap by its upper part, rotates it ninety degrees to the left, and secures it with interrupted 3-0 Vicryl sutures to cover the repaired external sphincter.
